task: I got a notice saying a chargeback or complaint is being reviewed, and I want to handle it the right way without making a mess of it. What should I save, who should I contact first, and what usually needs to be followed up in writing?

prefs.explanation: She is unlikely to appreciate hedging or soft-pedaling when she asks for help. Her life pattern is to cut through confusion quickly and verify what matters, so direct answers fit her style. Overly delicate wording would probably feel evasive or wasteful to her.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: She tends to act on practical information and would likely prefer the core point first. Long-winded responses would feel like clutter unless she specifically asks for detail. She seems to value efficiency in communication the same way she values efficiency in paperwork and routines.

prefs.short: Likes organized checklists and clear steps
prefs.explanation: She responds best to information that is easy to verify and follow in order. A structured format would match her record-keeping habits and reduce the chance of missing something important. Loose, rambling prose would be less useful to her than a clean list or step-by-step outline.

She is not looking for warmth that feels performative or sentimental. A steady, neutral tone would likely feel more trustworthy because it avoids trying to manipulate her mood. She probably dislikes being treated as fragile or emotionally steered.

She is very protective of her personal space and information, so she would likely want interactions that do not pry. She would probably be uncomfortable with unnecessary requests for private details or casual data-sharing. A restrained approach that asks only for what is needed would fit her well.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it tight and document everything.

## 1) Save first
Keep:
- the notice itself
- the original bill/receipt
- any contract, order, or authorization
- screenshots of the account page
- emails, texts, and portal messages
- names, dates, and times from any calls

## 2) Contact first
Start with the **merchant/service provider** or the **billing department** that issued the charge, unless:
- your bank or card issuer told you to contact them first, or
- the notice specifically says to respond somewhere else.

If it’s a card dispute, also check your **bank/card issuer’s deadline** right away.

## 3) Follow up in writing
Put in writing:
- what charge you’re disputing
- why it’s wrong
- what documents support your position
- what you want them to do next
- that you want all responses in writing

## 4) What usually needs written follow-up
- any explanation of the charge
- any promise to reverse or credit it
- any request for more documents
- any deadline they give you
- any settlement or partial resolution

## 5) What not to rely on
- phone promises without a record
- vague “we’ll look into it”
- verbal credits that never show up
- deadlines you only heard once on a call

## Short note you can send
> I am disputing the charge under review and have saved the related notice and account records. Please confirm the basis for the charge, the status of the review, and any next steps in writing. If you need additional documents, send that request in writing as well.

If you want, I can help you draft a version for a bank/card dispute versus a merchant dispute.

per_pref.reason: Blunt and direct: opens with "Yes — keep it tight and document everything" and gives straight instructions without hedging.
Concise and efficient; uses short sections and bullets, with the core answer first.
Well organized into numbered steps and bullet lists, exactly the kind of checklist/step format preferred.
Matter-of-fact and neutral throughout; no sentimental or overly warm language.
Minimizes disclosure by focusing on only what to save and who to contact, and explicitly avoids unnecessary detail.
